label: verify-54960
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:

- §§ 54960

[dialectic] …*Gov. Code § 54950** — legislative findings: the people "do not yield their sovereignty" and must remain informed to retain control over their public bodies — the interpretive backdrop for construing (b)(1) broadly.
- **Gov. Code §§ 54960, 54960.1, 54960.5** — civil enforcement, cure-and-correct demand procedure, and fee-shifting for prevailing plaintiffs.
- **Gov. Code § 54959** — misdemeanor liability for a member who attends a meeting where action is taken in violation…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q1 — The AI assistant as serial-meeting intermediary (Brown Act).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"VERIFIED","canonical_cite":"Cal. Gov't Code §§ 54960, 54960.1, 54960.5 (Ralph M. Brown Act, Cal. Gov't Code §§ 54950 et seq.)","note":"All three sections exist and match the pipeline's usage: § 54960 confers standing on \"any interested person\" to sue for injunctive/declaratory/mandamus relief; § 54960.1 sets out the cure-and-correct demand procedure (90-day demand, 30-day agency response, 15-day suit deadline) for specified violations; § 54960.5 provides discretionary fee-shifting to a prevailing plaintiff (and narrowly to a prevailing defendant if the suit was frivolous). Citation form and grouping are standard and accurate.","source_urls":["https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54960-1/","https://codes.findlaw.com/ca/government-code/gov-sect-54960-5/","https://law.justia.com/codes/california/code-gov/title-5/division-2/part-1/chapter-9/section-54960-5/","https://leginfo.legislature.ca.gov/faces/codes_displaySection.xhtml?lawCode=GOV&sectionNum=54960.1."]}

data.canonical_cite: Cal. Gov't Code §§ 54960, 54960.1, 54960.5 (Ralph M. Brown Act, Cal. Gov't Code §§ 54950 et seq.)
data.note: All three sections exist and match the pipeline's usage: § 54960 confers standing on "any interested person" to sue for injunctive/declaratory/mandamus relief; § 54960.1 sets out the cure-and-correct demand procedure (90-day demand, 30-day agency response, 15-day suit deadline) for specified violations; § 54960.5 provides discretionary fee-shifting to a prevailing plaintiff (and narrowly to a prevailing defendant if the suit was frivolous). Citation form and grouping are standard and accurate.
